title: Should photographs of a disaster be allowed to fade?
summary: Argue whether the images of a catastrophe should be endlessly preserved and reshared, or allowed to soften and recede the way a community's grief eventually does.
topic: Whether the photographic record of a disaster should be permanently preserved or allowed to fade as the people who lived it recover
prompt: Write a 450–600-word argumentative essay on whether the photographs of a disaster — a flood, a fire, an attack — should be preserved and recirculated forever, or whether a community has a right to let those images fade as it heals. Argue the tension between the duty to remember and the right not to be defined by one's worst day. Give two concrete examples and concede one objection. Use cleft sentences, the passive voice, and concession markers.
hintStarters: What an image preserves is not only the event but the moment a place was most…
Granted, a community that forgets its disaster may be condemned to…
It is the survivors, more than the historians, who must live inside the picture that…
We treat the duty to remember as absolute, when the people remembered may have a right to…

hintVocab.meaningVi: lưu hành lại / phát tán lại
bị định danh bởi / bị quy về
cảnh tượng để mọi người xem
sự thanh toán / đối diện với quá khứ
khiến trở thành bất tử / vĩnh viễn hóa
còn nguyên vết thương / chưa nguôi

sampleText:
After the flood that drowned a third of their town, the people of Lynmouth lived for decades beside a photograph: a single muddy street, a car on its roof, a woman staring into the camera with a face that everyone in Britain came to recognise. She had not asked to be the picture. Yet there she remained, reprinted on every anniversary, while the town behind her quietly rebuilt itself into a place she might have wanted to be remembered for instead.

The question is sharper than it first appears. What an image preserves is not only the event but the moment a place was most broken, most exposed, least itself — and an image, once preserved, tends to be the one thing that does not recover when everything else does. The street is rebuilt; the photograph of the ruined street is not. It is fixed, recirculated, and handed to people who were not born when the water came, until the town's worst hour becomes the only hour the world will agree to look at.

Consider how a disaster archive ages. A picture taken to bear witness becomes, within a generation, a stock image — searched, licensed, dropped into documentaries about something else entirely. The grieving woman is now a symbol, her name lost, her consent never sought. Or consider the families of a factory fire in Dhaka who asked, years later, that a particular photograph of the bodies be retired from the news cycle; they were told it was now 'historically important', as though importance were a reason to overrule the people it depicted. It is the survivors, more than the historians, who must live inside the picture that the rest of the world merely files.

Granted, a community that forgets its disaster may be condemned to suffer it again, and there is a real danger in letting images soften into nothing. Some photographs must not fade: the ones that fix responsibility, that stop a state from denying what it did, that a court or a future generation will need. To let those go would not be healing but erasure, and it would betray the dead more deeply than any reprint. The objection is serious and cannot be dismissed.

Even so, not every photograph carries that weight, and the duty to remember has been allowed to swallow every other claim. We treat the duty to remember as absolute, when the people remembered may have a right to recede — to be more than the worst thing that ever happened to them, to grow old without their faces frozen at the moment of their greatest helplessness. There is a difference between an archive that can be consulted and an image that will not stop circulating, and that difference is exactly where a community's dignity lives.

What should fade is not the memory but its endless, unasked-for display. A disaster can be remembered in a record that waits quietly to be opened, rather than a picture that arrives uninvited every year on the phones of the people who lived it. The dead are honoured by being remembered, not by being shown. And the living, who carry the memory in their bodies whether or not anyone preserves it, are owed the smaller mercy of a day that is, at last, allowed to belong to the present.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: It-cleft to put survivors ahead of historians
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is the survivors, more than the historians, who must live inside the picture' dùng câu chẻ it-cleft để đẩy người sống sót lên hàng đầu, làm nổi bật rằng quyền lợi của họ phải được cân nhắc trước tiên.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the survivors, more than the historians, who must live inside the picture' uses an it-cleft to foreground the survivors, stressing that their claim must come first.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to show the image acting on people without consent
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is fixed, recirculated, and handed to people …', 'her consent never sought', và 'be retired from the news cycle' dùng bị động để cho thấy bức ảnh được lưu hành và áp đặt lên con người mà không có sự đồng ý của họ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is fixed, recirculated, and handed to people …', 'her consent never sought', and 'be retired from the news cycle' use the passive to show the image being circulated and imposed on people without their consent.

sampleTranslationVi:
Sau trận lụt nhấn chìm một phần ba thị trấn của họ, người dân Lynmouth đã sống suốt nhiều thập kỷ bên cạnh một bức ảnh: một con phố lầy lội duy nhất, một chiếc xe lật ngửa, một người phụ nữ nhìn thẳng vào ống kính với một gương mặt mà cả nước Anh dần nhận ra. Bà đâu có xin được trở thành bức ảnh ấy. Vậy mà bà cứ ở đó, được in lại vào mỗi dịp kỷ niệm, trong khi thị trấn phía sau bà lặng lẽ xây lại chính mình thành một nơi mà có lẽ bà đã muốn được nhớ đến vì nó hơn.

Câu hỏi này sắc bén hơn vẻ ngoài ban đầu của nó. Cái mà một bức ảnh lưu giữ không chỉ là sự kiện mà là khoảnh khắc một nơi tan vỡ nhất, phơi bày nhất, ít là chính mình nhất — và một bức ảnh, một khi đã được lưu giữ, có xu hướng là thứ duy nhất không hồi phục khi mọi thứ khác hồi phục. Con phố được xây lại; bức ảnh con phố đổ nát thì không. Nó bị đóng cứng, được phát tán lại, và trao cho những người còn chưa ra đời khi nước tràn tới, cho đến khi giờ phút tồi tệ nhất của thị trấn trở thành giờ phút duy nhất mà thế giới chịu nhìn vào.

Hãy xét xem một kho lưu trữ thảm họa già đi như thế nào. Một bức ảnh chụp để làm chứng trở thành, chỉ trong một thế hệ, một bức ảnh kho — được tìm kiếm, được mua bản quyền, được thả vào những bộ phim tài liệu về một thứ hoàn toàn khác. Người phụ nữ đang đau buồn giờ là một biểu tượng, tên bà thất lạc, sự đồng ý của bà chưa bao giờ được hỏi đến. Hoặc hãy xét đến gia đình của các nạn nhân một vụ cháy nhà máy ở Dhaka, những người đã yêu cầu, nhiều năm sau, rằng một bức ảnh cụ thể về các thi thể được rút khỏi vòng quay tin tức; họ được bảo rằng giờ nó đã 'quan trọng về mặt lịch sử', như thể tầm quan trọng là một lý do để gạt bỏ ý nguyện của chính những người mà nó khắc họa. Chính những người sống sót, hơn cả các nhà sử học, mới phải sống bên trong bức ảnh mà phần còn lại của thế giới chỉ đơn thuần lưu hồ sơ.

Công nhận, một cộng đồng quên đi thảm họa của mình có thể bị buộc phải chịu đựng nó lần nữa, và có một mối nguy thực sự trong việc để những bức ảnh dịu xuống thành hư không. Một số bức ảnh không được phép phai mờ: những bức quy trách nhiệm, ngăn một nhà nước chối bỏ điều nó đã làm, những bức mà một tòa án hay một thế hệ tương lai sẽ cần đến. Buông bỏ những bức ấy sẽ không phải là chữa lành mà là sự xóa bỏ, và nó sẽ phản bội người chết sâu sắc hơn bất kỳ lần in lại nào. Phản đối này là nghiêm túc và không thể bị gạt bỏ.

Dẫu vậy, không phải bức ảnh nào cũng mang sức nặng ấy, và bổn phận phải nhớ đã được phép nuốt chửng mọi quyền lợi khác. Chúng ta coi bổn phận phải nhớ là tuyệt đối, trong khi những người được nhớ đến có thể có quyền được lùi xa — được là gì đó nhiều hơn điều tồi tệ nhất từng xảy đến với họ, được già đi mà gương mặt không bị đóng băng ở khoảnh khắc bất lực nhất của mình. Có một sự khác biệt giữa một kho lưu trữ có thể được tra cứu và một bức ảnh không chịu ngừng lan truyền, và sự khác biệt ấy chính là nơi phẩm giá của một cộng đồng cư ngụ.

Cái nên phai mờ không phải là ký ức mà là sự phô bày bất tận, không ai yêu cầu của nó. Một thảm họa có thể được nhớ đến trong một hồ sơ lặng lẽ chờ được mở ra, thay vì một bức ảnh đến không mời mỗi năm trên điện thoại của chính những người đã sống qua nó. Người chết được tôn vinh bằng việc được nhớ đến, chứ không phải bằng việc bị đem ra cho xem. Và những người sống, những kẻ mang ký ức trong chính cơ thể mình dù có ai lưu giữ nó hay không, xứng đáng được nhận ân huệ nhỏ hơn là một ngày mà cuối cùng được phép thuộc về hiện tại.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to put the survivors ahead of the historians?
options: The survivors must live it is more than the historians inside the picture who files.
More than the historians the picture it is who must the survivors live inside.
Who must live inside the picture it is the historians more than the survivors files.
It is the survivors, more than the historians, who must live inside the picture that the rest of the world merely files.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X, more than Y, who …' is an it-cleft foregrounding the survivors over the historians.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is the survivors … who …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to show the image circulating without consent?
options: It is fixed, recirculated, and handed to people who were not born when the water came.
The woman happily shares her own photograph everywhere she goes.
She personally hands the picture to every new generation herself.
Handed recirculated fixed is it to people the water when came.
mcQuestions.explanation: The passive verbs ('is fixed, recirculated, and handed') show the image acted upon and spread without her agency.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses passive verbs with no named actor?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence concedes the counter-argument most fairly?
options: Obviously remembering disasters is pointless and harmful.
Granted, a community that forgets its disaster may be condemned to suffer it again.
Of course nobody should ever look at any old photograph.
Naturally, history is a waste of everyone's time.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' honestly admits the danger of forgetting, the strongest case against the writer.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits the real risk of letting memory fade?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to be defined by' mean as used here?
options: To be explained in a dictionary
To be honoured with a memorial
To be reduced to and known only for one thing
To be photographed clearly
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay worries that survivors are reduced to, and known only for, their worst day.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of being known for nothing but one event.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from the concession back to the argument?
options: In conclusion, we should burn every disaster photograph.
On the contrary, memory is always worthless.
Anyway, photographs do not matter to anyone.
Even so, not every photograph carries that weight, and the duty to remember has been allowed to swallow every other claim.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' accepts that some images must be kept, then limits the duty's reach.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase grants the prior point and then qualifies it?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent argumentative register?
options: There is a difference between an archive that can be consulted and an image that will not stop circulating.
Honestly it's just so messed up that they keep posting those photos, right?
Basically the news people are vultures and that's the whole story, period.
It kind of makes me sick seeing those pics over and over, you know?
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B draws a precise distinction; the others slip into casual or accusatory speech.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'messed up', 'basically', and 'kind of makes me sick'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the central tension the essay argues?
options: Between the cost of cameras and the cost of memorials
Between the duty to remember and the right not to be defined by one's worst day
Between historians and journalists
Between old photographs and new ones
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay sets the duty to remember against survivors' right to recede.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the fifth paragraph's framing of the conflict.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which photographs does the essay say must NOT fade?
options: The ones that win awards
The ones that are most beautiful
The ones that fix responsibility and stop a state from denying what it did
The ones that sell the most copies
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay concedes that images fixing responsibility must be kept; that is the honest concession.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the images a court or future generation will need.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most effectively?
options: the moment a place was most broken, exposing it, and itself the least
the moment most broken a place was, to expose, and being least itself
the moment a place broken most was, the exposing most, itself the least being
the moment a place was most broken, most exposed, least itself
mcQuestions.explanation: Three matched superlative phrases form a clean parallel description.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all three phrases in the same form?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the Dhaka factory-fire example illustrate?
options: That 'historical importance' can be used to override the wishes of the people depicted
That photographs are always inaccurate
That fires are more dangerous than floods
That families never want anything preserved
mcQuestions.explanation: The families were told the image was 'historically important' as a reason to ignore their request.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread what the families were told when they asked.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses a semicolon to join two contrasting clauses?
options: The street is; rebuilt the photograph of the ruined street is not.
The street is rebuilt; the photograph of the ruined street is not.
The street rebuilt is the photograph; of the ruined street is not.
The street is rebuilt the photograph; of the ruined; street is not.
mcQuestions.explanation: A semicolon correctly joins two complete, contrasting clauses.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places the semicolon between two full sentences?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's final position?
options: All disaster photographs should be destroyed
Survivors have no say over their own images
The memory should remain, but its endless, unasked-for display should be allowed to fade
Photographs are more important than the people in them
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay distinguishes the memory (kept) from the constant display (allowed to fade).
mcQuestions.hint: Look for what the essay says 'should fade'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'consent' mean as used here?
options: Payment for a photograph
A historical record
A type of memorial service
Permission willingly given by the person involved
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Consent' is the permission the photographed woman was never asked to give.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of permission that should have been sought.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best captures the essay's closing distinction?
options: The dead are honoured by being remembered, not by being shown.
The dead and the living both want to be forgotten completely.
Showing photographs is the only way to honour anyone.
Memory and display are exactly the same thing.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B states the closing distinction between honouring through memory and exposing through display.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option keeps the concession honest rather than dismissive?
options: Anyone who wants to preserve photographs is just cruel.
To let those go would not be healing but erasure, and it would betray the dead more deeply than any reprint.
Forgetting a disaster never causes any harm whatsoever.
No photograph has ever held a government to account.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B genuinely grants that some images must stay, which keeps the argument credible.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits that letting some images go would be wrong?

options: To grow louder and more visible
To photograph repeatedly
To move back, withdraw, or grow less prominent over time
To rebuild after a disaster
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To recede' means to withdraw or become less prominent — what the essay says survivors have a right to do.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of something gradually drawing back and fading.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If a photograph of you on the worst day of your life became famous, would you want it preserved forever as a historical record, or allowed to fade — and does your answer change when it is someone else's worst day being remembered instead of your own?
